domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: During traffic spikes, an app's telemetry shows a rising count of HTTP 429 responses from Azure Cosmos DB. What does a 429 indicate, and what is the appropriate first response?

options.text: The request rate exceeded the provisioned RU/s budget on a partition; increase throughput (or enable autoscale) and/or fix a hot partition key.
The request was unauthorized; rotate the account keys.
A transient write conflict occurred; permanently abandon the operation.
The container was not found; recreate it.

questions.explanation: A 429 means the per-second RU budget for a physical partition was exhausted; the SDK auto-retries, but sustained 429s call for more RU/s or a better partition key.

questions.stem: Normalized RU Consumption is pinned at 100% and clients receive 429s, yet the account's total RU usage is far below its provisioned throughput. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The account is under-provisioned overall.
A network outage between regions.
A single hot partition, because the metric is the maximum utilization across all partition key ranges.
TTL is deleting documents too quickly.

questions.explanation: Normalized RU Consumption is a maximum (not an average) across partition key ranges, so one overloaded partition can pin it at 100% while total account usage stays low.

questions.stem: Clients observe about 120 ms request latency, but the Azure Monitor server-side latency metric reports about 4 ms for the same operations. Where should you focus your investigation?

options.text: The Cosmos DB indexing policy.
The network, SDK configuration, or client resource pressure, because server-side latency excludes network round-trip.
The backup policy.
The partition key storage size.

questions.explanation: The server-side latency metric measures time inside the service and excludes network time, so a large client-vs-server gap points to the network, gateway mode, or client-side pressure.

questions.stem: You must be notified automatically whenever the account performs a regional failover. Which monitoring construct is the best fit?

options.text: A metric alert on Normalized RU Consumption.
A log alert on DataPlaneRequests.
An alert on the "Region failed over" activity/metric signal.
A CORS configuration change notification.

questions.explanation: A regional failover is surfaced as an activity/metric event that alerting can watch, unlike RU or request-level data-plane signals.

questions.stem: Which Azure Cosmos DB alerting scenarios are best implemented as activity-log alerts rather than metric alerts? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Account keys were rotated.
Requests are being throttled (429).
A region failed over.
Normalized RU Consumption exceeds 90%.

questions.explanation: Key rotation and region failover are control-plane events surfaced in the activity log, whereas throttling and RU utilization are metric-based alerts.

questions.stem: True or false: Platform metrics for an Azure Cosmos DB account are collected automatically, but resource logs must be explicitly enabled with a diagnostic setting before you can query them.
questions.explanation: Metrics flow automatically at one-minute granularity, whereas resource logs require a diagnostic setting routing them to Log Analytics, a storage account, or Event Hubs.

questions.stem: You suspect one tenant's logical partition key is consuming most of your container's RUs. Which resource log category confirms which keys burn the most throughput?

options.text: PartitionKeyStatistics
ControlPlaneRequests
QueryRuntimeStatistics
PartitionKeyRUConsumption

questions.explanation: PartitionKeyRUConsumption aggregates RU charge per logical partition key, exposing hot keys; PartitionKeyStatistics reports storage instead.

questions.stem: Which resource log category reports the storage size of your largest logical partition keys so you can detect a key approaching its hard limit, and what is that limit?

options.text: PartitionKeyRUConsumption; 10 GB
PartitionKeyStatistics; 20 GB
DataPlaneRequests; 50 GB
ControlPlaneRequests; 20 GB

questions.explanation: PartitionKeyStatistics reports per-logical-partition storage size, and a logical partition is capped at 20 GB, after which further writes for that key fail.

questions.stem: Your security team needs an audit trail of every throughput change, firewall/network change, and key operation on the account. Which resource log category should you enable?

options.text: DataPlaneRequests
PartitionKeyStatistics
ControlPlaneRequests
QueryRuntimeStatistics

questions.explanation: ControlPlaneRequests audits management operations such as throughput, firewall/network, and key changes; DataPlaneRequests audits document operations.

questions.stem: Auditors require the ability to restore data to the exact second before an accidental delete, using self-service with no support ticket. Which backup mode must the account use?

options.text: Periodic backup with the default 4-hour interval.
Continuous backup with point-in-time restore.
Periodic backup with locally redundant storage.
Manual export to Blob Storage via Data Factory.

questions.explanation: Only continuous backup offers self-service point-in-time restore to any second within the 7- or 30-day window; periodic-mode restores require a Microsoft support ticket.

questions.stem: After a periodic-mode restore into a new account, which of the following are NOT carried over and must be reconfigured? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Account keys (new ones are generated).
The documents that existed at the backup time.
Firewall, VNet, and private-endpoint configuration.
RBAC role assignments and stored procedures/triggers/UDFs.

questions.explanation: A restore recreates the data into a new single-region account but does not bring back keys, network rules, RBAC assignments, or server-side scripts.

questions.stem: True or false: After migrating an Azure Cosmos DB account from periodic to continuous backup, you can later switch it back to periodic backup.
questions.explanation: The periodic-to-continuous migration is one-way and cannot be reversed.

questions.stem: Before initiating a point-in-time restore, you want to confirm the most recent moment you can restore to for a container. What should you retrieve?

options.text: The latest restorable timestamp for the container.
The account's provisioned RU/s.
The container's indexing policy version.
The Normalized RU Consumption value.

questions.explanation: The latest restorable timestamp defines the most recent valid restore point within the retention window; the event feed helps locate a moment just before a destructive change.

questions.stem: After a continuous-backup restore, documents with a short TTL disappear almost immediately from the new account. How do you preserve them on the next restore?

options.text: Increase the RU/s before restoring.
Restore into the same source account in place.
Restore with --disable-ttl True.
Enable analytical store on the container.

questions.explanation: TTL is restored by default, so aged documents expire on arrival unless you pass --disable-ttl True during the restore.

questions.stem: A compliance policy requires your organization to control and be able to revoke the encryption key protecting data at rest. Which option satisfies this?

options.text: Disable encryption at rest and manage it in the application.
Service-managed keys.
Always Encrypted deterministic fields.
Customer-managed keys (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault.

questions.explanation: Encryption at rest is always on; CMK adds a customer-controlled key layer in Key Vault with rotation and revocation control, which service-managed keys do not provide.

questions.stem: Which mechanisms can you use to restrict from where requests are allowed to reach an Azure Cosmos DB account? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: IP firewall (ipRules / CIDR ranges).
VNet service endpoints for specific subnets.
Private endpoints (Private Link).
A container-level CORS allowedOrigins list.
An Always Encrypted encryption policy.

questions.explanation: The IP firewall, VNet service endpoints, and Private Link private endpoints all control network reachability; CORS governs browser origins and Always Encrypted protects fields, not network access.

questions.stem: With customer-managed keys (CMK) enabled, which statement is true about what the customer key protects?

options.text: It double-encrypts the main transactional store, but container names and partition key values (metadata) are not encrypted with the CMK.
It encrypts all metadata, including container and stored-procedure names.
It double-encrypts the Synapse Link analytical store.
It replaces the automatic AES-256 encryption at rest.

questions.explanation: CMK double-encrypts stored transactional data, but metadata such as names, index paths, and partition key values — plus the analytical store and continuous backups — fall outside CMK.

questions.stem: You need a team to create databases and change throughput on an account but never read account keys, connection strings, or documents. Which built-in role should you assign?

options.text: Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor
Cosmos DB Operator
Owner
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader

questions.explanation: The control-plane Cosmos DB Operator role manages accounts and resources while explicitly blocking access to keys, connection strings, and data.

questions.stem: A user with the Reader role can browse containers in Data Explorer, but with account keys disabled they get errors when reading items. What is missing?

options.text: A CORS allowedOrigins entry.
A private endpoint.
A data-plane RBAC role assignment (for example, Data Reader).
The Cosmos DB Operator role.

questions.explanation: Control-plane RBAC lets a user see resources in Data Explorer, but reading or writing data without keys additionally requires a data-plane RBAC assignment.

questions.stem: The application must read and write documents with no account keys anywhere in code, using least-privilege access. Which combination meets this requirement?

options.text: Store the primary key in Key Vault and read it with a managed identity.
Assign the Cosmos DB Operator role to the app.
Assign the Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor role to the app's Entra identity and set disableLocalAuth = true.
Enable CORS and use a wildcard origin.

questions.explanation: Data-plane RBAC grants keyless read/write through Microsoft Entra ID, and disableLocalAuth = true eliminates key-based access entirely.

questions.stem: A single-page app at https://app.contoso.com is blocked by a CORS error when calling the API for NoSQL. Which statement about resolving this is correct?

options.text: CORS is unavailable for Cosmos DB; use a backend proxy only.
Add the exact origin to the account's CORS allowedOrigins list; per-subdomain wildcards like https://*.contoso.com are not supported.
CORS applies to all Cosmos DB APIs equally.
Set publicNetworkAccess = Disabled to fix the CORS error.

questions.explanation: CORS is supported for the API for NoSQL; you list exact origins (or *), but sub-domain wildcard patterns are not accepted.

questions.stem: Account keys are still required by a legacy component, but they must never appear in source code or configuration. What is the recommended pattern?

options.text: Store the key as an Azure Key Vault secret and retrieve it at runtime with a managed identity.
Hard-code the key but obfuscate it.
Email the key to the operations team.
Store the key in an app setting in plain text.

questions.explanation: Storing keys as Key Vault secrets fetched via managed identity keeps secrets out of code/config and centralizes rotation; disabling keys and using Entra ID is stronger still.

questions.stem: You are enabling customer-managed keys (CMK) for a new Cosmos DB account. Which of the following are required for the setup to succeed? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Azure Key Vault must have Soft Delete enabled.
Azure Key Vault must have Purge Protection enabled.
Cosmos DB must be granted Get / WrapKey / UnwrapKey on the key.
The account must use the API for Cassandra.
Encryption at rest must first be manually turned on.

questions.explanation: CMK requires a Key Vault with Soft Delete and Purge Protection, and the Cosmos DB principal must have Get/WrapKey/UnwrapKey (or the Key Vault Crypto Service Encryption User role).

questions.stem: You must encrypt an SSN field with Always Encrypted while still supporting equality (exact-match) queries on it. Which encryption type do you choose?

options.text: Randomized
Deterministic
Service-managed key
Transparent data encryption

questions.explanation: Deterministic encryption produces identical ciphertext for identical plaintext, enabling equality filters; randomized is stronger but not queryable.

questions.stem: Which data-movement tools are appropriate for continuous, streaming ingestion into or out of Azure Cosmos DB? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Apache Kafka connector.
Azure Stream Analytics output sink.
Azure IoT Hub custom endpoint routing.
SDK bulk import (AllowBulkExecution).
Azure Data Factory scheduled Copy activity.

questions.explanation: Kafka, Stream Analytics, and IoT Hub routing handle streaming scenarios; SDK bulk suits one-off high-volume imports and Data Factory suits scheduled no-code ETL.

questions.stem: You must import millions of documents from custom .NET code as fast as possible. How do you enable bulk mode, and when is the setting fixed?

options.text: Set AllowBulkExecution = true per request; it can change at any time.
Call a BulkImport() method on the container.
Set AllowBulkExecution = true in CosmosClientOptions at client creation; it is immutable afterward.
Enable it in the account's ARM template.

questions.explanation: AllowBulkExecution = true is set on CosmosClientOptions at construction and can't change later; the SDK then batches concurrent operations per partition.

questions.stem: You need a scheduled, no-code export of Cosmos DB data to Azure Blob Storage, and later to also load data back in. What is true of the Azure Data Factory/Synapse Cosmos DB connector?

options.text: It works as both a source and a sink, supporting scheduled copy in and out.
It can only read from Cosmos DB, never write.
It only supports streaming, not scheduled copies.
It requires custom code for each direction.

questions.explanation: The connector is bidirectional (source and sink) and supports no-code, schedulable copy activities in and out of Cosmos DB.

questions.stem: Which statements about the Azure Cosmos DB Kafka Connect connectors are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The sink connector writes Kafka topic records into Cosmos DB.
The source connector reads the Cosmos DB change feed and publishes to Kafka.
The containers.topicmap setting maps each topic to a container using topic#container.
The source connector writes into Cosmos DB using the transactional store.

questions.explanation: Sink means Kafka to Cosmos DB; source reads the change feed to Kafka; containers.topicmap pairs topics with containers.

questions.stem: A Stream Analytics job cannot write to Cosmos DB because of the account's IP firewall. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Add the job's static outbound IP to the firewall.
Enable "Accept connections from within Azure datacenters" (0.0.0.0), because Stream Analytics has no fixed IP.
Disable public network access on the account.
Switch the account to periodic backup.

questions.explanation: Stream Analytics has no static IP to allowlist, so you permit Azure-internal traffic; the sink then upserts documents by id.

questions.stem: You want to run heavy Spark analytics over Cosmos DB data without consuming transactional RUs, then write curated results back into a container. Which Spark data source formats do you use?

options.text: Read with cosmos.oltp; write with cosmos.olap.
Read with cosmos.olap (analytical store, RU-free); write with cosmos.oltp (transactional store).
Read and write both with cosmos.oltp.
Read and write both with cosmos.olap.

questions.explanation: cosmos.olap reads the RU-free analytical store via Synapse Link, while cosmos.oltp reads/writes the transactional store (consuming RUs).

questions.stem: Device messages routed by IoT Hub are landing in Cosmos DB as base64 strings instead of JSON. What did the devices fail to set on the messages?

options.text: A synthetic partition key template.
The Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader role.
A CORS allowedOrigins entry.
contentType = application/json and contentEncoding = UTF-8.

questions.explanation: Without contentType = application/json and contentEncoding = UTF-8, IoT Hub stores the message body base64-encoded rather than as JSON.

questions.stem: A team wants identical Cosmos DB environments provisioned repeatably and reviewably through a release pipeline. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Declarative ARM/Bicep templates, which are idempotent and version-controlled.
Imperative Azure CLI scripts run manually per environment.
Portal clicks documented in a runbook.
SDK calls from application startup.

questions.explanation: Declarative tools describe the desired end state and are idempotent, repeatable, and source-controllable, making them ideal for CI/CD and environment parity.

questions.stem: You want a container's indexing policy defined in source control and deployed with the rest of the infrastructure. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Indexing policy can only be set through the portal.
Include indexingPolicy in the ARM/Bicep template and deploy via the pipeline, alongside throughput, firewall, RBAC, and backup policy.
ARM templates can only create the account shell, not indexing policy.
Indexing policy must be set with the SDK at runtime.

questions.explanation: ARM/Bicep templates manage indexing policy declaratively along with the account, databases, containers, throughput, firewall, CORS, RBAC, and backup policy.

questions.stem: Switching a container between manual (standard) and autoscale throughput is supported through which of the following? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The Azure portal.
Azure CLI.
PowerShell.
An ARM/Bicep template deployment.
The client SDK.

questions.explanation: Throughput-type migration is imperative-only — portal, CLI, or PowerShell — and cannot be performed via an ARM/Bicep template or the SDK.

questions.stem: You need to deliberately promote West US to the write region of a single-write-region account for a planned test. Which action accomplishes this?

options.text: Run az cosmosdb failover-priority-change with westus=0, making West US priority 0 (the write region).
Delete and recreate the account in West US.
Change the default consistency level.
Set publicNetworkAccess = Disabled.

questions.explanation: Failover priority 0 is always the write region, so setting westus=0 promotes West US via az cosmosdb failover-priority-change.

questions.stem: You deploy a new indexing policy to a production container through your CI/CD pipeline. What is the impact on write availability?

options.text: Writes are blocked until the transformation completes.
The container must be recreated.
None — the index transformation runs online, so production queries and writes keep working.
The account fails over to another region.

questions.explanation: Index changes trigger an online transformation applied asynchronously in place, so there is no write-availability downtime.
